EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One last colder and quick-moving storm wrapped up the current active storm cycle during the overnight/early morning hours of January 18-19. This winter system produced 4-10 inches of accumulating snow across much of the Sierra and Lake Tahoe Basin with lesser amounts across northeast California and around the Reno-Sparks area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at the Mount Rose Ski Base, and a trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, NV, reported 24 hour snowfall totals of 9.0 and 7.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December finished up early on New Year's day.  This atmospheric river (AR) enhanced winter storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December with heavy rainfall continuing below that elevation.  But colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Year���s Eve that carried over into the overnight hours through early morning New Year���s day. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus of new snow with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus of accumulating snow above 7000 feet before the winter system exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer (DAGN2) located at Dagget Pass, NV, as well as the public (via social media) located 2.0 miles SE of Gardnerville, NV, and 10.0 mi E of Gardnerville Ranch reported storm total snowfall of 31, 30, and 25 inches respectively during the period from 12/31/22 through 1/1/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inside slider brought an additional round of snow to the region on January 29th. The cold, easterly flow with this system allowed for the better orographic enhancement of modest mid-level moisture east of the Sierra crest.  Parts of far western NV and northeast California received the most snowfall, with 1-4 inches of storm total snow across the greater Reno and Carson City area as well as areas south into the Carson valley of Douglas County along US-395. Areas across northeast California east of the Sierra Crest received around 1-3 inches of accumulating snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at the Mount Rose Ski summit, as well as COCORAHS stations NV-WH-167  located 3.5 mi SSW of Reno, NV, and NVNV-DG-23 located 5.7 mi W of Gardnerville, NV, reported storm total snowfall of 7.0, 6.3, and 4.7 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm produced several waves of new snow and high winds that impacted the region during the third week of January. A relatively long-duration atmospheric river from 9-11 January supported forcing and moisture for the first in the series of winter systems that brought heavy Sierra snowfall with accumulations ranging from 3-6 feet across the Sierra Crest. The heaviest snowfall occurred in Mono County over the 3-day period with heavy, unrelenting snowfall that forced a prolonged closure of US-395 from Bridgeport to Bishop from late morning January 9th through the afternoon January 11th.  On January 9th, heavy rain across the Lake Tahoe Basin changed to heavy snow below 7000 ft, leading to snowfall totals ranging from 8-20 inches through January 11th. Over 2 feet of snowfall was observed in parts of northeast California above 5000 feet. On January 9th mostly rain fell across western Nevada with storm total precipitation amounts ranging between 0.75-2 inches across much of Douglas, Carson City, southern Washoe, Mineral, and Lyon counties. The heavier rainfall across those contributed to a major rockfall and subsequently lead to an extended road closure on SR-208 in Wilson Canyon (Figure 4). Reports of areal flooding included I-80 near Lockwood, NN, Geiger Grade at Toll Rd in the Virginia City highlands, and along US-395 south of Reno with flooding from Steamboat Creek spilling over Towne Road. The changeover to snow in western Nevada arrived during the morning commute on January 10th which led to several related traffic incidents that included a 19-car pile-up on the Galena Bridge on I-580.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station (NV DOT-REV) located along US-95 near Walker Lake, NV reported a peak wind gust from the south at 75 mph at 11:57 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of AR events brought valley rain, mountain snow, and high winds. Due to the valley rain, some snowmelt occurred leading to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures, rainfall, and snowmelt caused flooding of O'Neill Basin Road 755, as well as Twin Bridges road 715B. These roads were closed. Additionally, there were reports of flooding (standing water) for: All Osino Roads, All Ryndon Roads, Spring Valley Parkway and Spring Valley Court (orange traffic barrels were in place). Coal Mine Canyon Road, Burner Basin Roads (rutted roads and retaining water), Last Chance Road (flowing water over roadway at Buzzard. Contact Road by NDOT Station (washout). Spring Creek Parkway (several areas of pooling water). Dirt roads around Elko County were noted as muddy, rutted, and holding water in various areas. In addition to the above the following roads also had some minor flooding: West Bullion Road, Lowe South Fork Road, Lucky Nugget Road, Lower Lamoille Road, Fort Halleck Road, Old Devils Gate Road, Elburz Road, and Kittridge Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system brought active weather to parts of Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter Weather caused a Multi-Vehicle crash on I-80 near MM268 (Emigrant Pass). The Crash initially involved 2 semis. The fuel cell of one of the semis became breached, spilling over 25 gallons of Diesel onto the highway, and into a water catch basin. At least 3 additional accidents occurred after the initial crash which closed the westbound lanes for several hours. Eureka County Sheriffs Office and Nevada State Police had to shut down the eastbound lanes in order to shuttle emergency vehicles. Multiple people were transported to Northeastern Regional Hospital for their injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger two-part storm aided by an enhanced influx of subtropical moisture from a moderate atmospheric river continued the current series of Pacific winter storms passing through the region January 14-16. The initial thrust of AR enhanced moisture with the first storm brought heavy snowfall to the Sierra on 14 January with 2 to 3 feet of accumulating snow across higher elevations above 7000 feet.  An additional 15-30 inches of new snow fell across the California side of the Lake Tahoe Basin below 7000 feet with 8-16 inches falling on the Nevada side. Periods of heavy snow forced the closure of Sierra passes with the primary Sierra travel routes (I-80, US-50) being closed for much of the day. Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 4.0 mi SSW of Reno, NV,  reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 7.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December finished up early on New Year's day.  This atmospheric river (AR) enhanced winter storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December with heavy rainfall continuing below that elevation.  But colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Year���s Eve that carried over into the overnight hours through early morning New Year���s day. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus of new snow with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus of accumulating snow above 7000 feet before the winter system exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at the Mount Rose Ski Base and Diamond Peak Ski Base both reported Storm total snowfall of 40 inches during the period from 12/31/22 through 1/1/23.

Precipitation started as rain for western Nevada on the 14th with rain totals of around a quarter inch and up to three-quarter inches for Reno-Carson City areas that continued south into the Carson Valley of Douglas County. Rainfall amounts with this storm surpassed the average water year precipitation of 7.35 inches on January 14th for the Reno area.  The second, colder wave of the same winter storm arrived overnight January 15-16.  This colder system produced 4-8 inches of total storm snowfall across the Reno-Carson metro areas including parts of Lyon-Mineral counties.  This system also dumped around a foot of snow across Douglas County. This second storm caused more significant travel impacts for western Nevada valleys/foothills.  Mono County and the Tahoe Basin also received an additional foot of accumulating snow accumulation with this colder follow-up to the winter storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, NV,  reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 12.5 inches with a 1.68 inch water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm produced several waves of new snow and high winds that impacted the region during the third week of January. A relatively long-duration atmospheric river from 9-11 January supported forcing and moisture for the first in the series of winter systems that brought heavy Sierra snowfall with accumulations ranging from 3-6 feet across the Sierra Crest. The heaviest snowfall occurred in Mono County over the 3-day period with heavy, unrelenting snowfall that forced a prolonged closure of US-395 from Bridgeport to Bishop from late morning January 9th through the afternoon January 11th.  On January 9th, heavy rain across the Lake Tahoe Basin changed to heavy snow below 7000 ft, leading to snowfall totals ranging from 8-20 inches through January 11th. Over 2 feet of snowfall was observed in parts of northeast California above 5000 feet. On January 9th mostly rain fell across western Nevada with storm total precipitation amounts ranging between 0.75-2 inches across much of Douglas, Carson City, southern Washoe, Mineral, and Lyon counties. The heavier rainfall across those contributed to a major rockfall and subsequently lead to an extended road closure on SR-208 in Wilson Canyon (Figure 4). Reports of areal flooding included I-80 near Lockwood, NN, Geiger Grade at Toll Rd in the Virginia City highlands, and along US-395 south of Reno with flooding from Steamboat Creek spilling over Towne Road. The changeover to snow in western Nevada arrived during the morning commute on January 10th which led to several related traffic incidents that included a 19-car pile-up on the Galena Bridge on I-580.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive landslide on State Route 208 through Wilson Canyon forced the closure of the highway for an indeterminate amount of time. The Nevada Department of Transportation (NDOT) says the rockslide was likely caused by the recent heavy precipitation. The debris fell across roughly 400 feet of roadway around 6:00 PM Tuesday night, NDOT estimates.||There were no immediate indications of any injuries due to the landslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another AR enhanced winter storm brought travel impacts to the region later Monday night through Wednesday, with strong winds, heavy Sierra snowfall, and periods of moderate valley rain or snow. A boom-bust scenario was projected to express the uncertainty of the track of a surface low track along the California coast. If the low tracked more south and offshore, this would limit the amount of moisture and mesoscale lift necessary for heavier snowfall across the region and lead to the higher chance bust scenario.  The lower probability boom scenario included if the low tracked closer to the Sierra Nevada and crossed into western Nevada. This projected track was expected to lead to stronger frontogenesis and moisture flux that would generate more persistent bands of heavier snowfall. With the high spread of potential scenarios, the official forecast split the difference.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public in Storey County, located 6.0 mi N of Virginia City, NV, reported a 24-hour snowfall total of 9.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The start of a more active weather pattern culminated in the passage of a Pacific storm into the region. The incoming cold front brought strong, gusty west-southwest winds, increased chances for more widespread moderate rain showers,  and mountain snow. Snow-levels dropping to or near higher Sierra pass level brought increased travel difficulties. An incoming cold front and strong Pacific jet stream sustained the gusty conditions across the Sierra ridges into western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 88 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system offshore the western US spun troughs over the region bringing periods of increased mountain snow and valley rain chances, as well as increased travel impacts through Sierra passes. Much of the precipitation was forecast to be confined to areas mostly south of I-80 with greatest snowfall staying along the Sierra crest in Alpine and Mono County. Rainfall totals were marginal for most lower elevation areas across western Nevada  outside of Mono and Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRahs station (NV-WH-161-Incline Village) located 1.3 mi S of Crystal Bay, CA, and a  CO-OP observer station (INVN2) located in 1.2 mi E of Incline Village, NV, reported 24 hour rainfall storm totals of 0.60 inches and 0.55 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (LCHN-Sheldon NWR) located 13.0 mi W of Catnip Mountain, NV, reported a rainfall storm total of 0.82 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 4.0 mi SSW of Reno, NV, reported a storm total snowfall of 8.5 inches. Another trained spotter located 3.0 WSW of Gardnerville Ranch, NV, as well as a CO-OP observer (MINN2) located at Minden, NV, both reported a storm total snowfall of 5.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night which again increased the chance for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base and 1.0 mi NE of the Diamond Peak Ski Base reported 24-hour snowfall totals of 5.0 and 8.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter AR driven storm brought stronger winds especially for mountain tops, heavy Sierra snowfall, and valley rain or snow showers through the forecast period. Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS located 2.0 mi NNW (Hawthorne Municipal Airport) of Hawthorne, NV, and mesonet station (WLKNV) located 6.1 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV,  and reported peak wind gusts of 76, and 60 mph, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, even damaging west-southwest winds increased travel hazards especially to high profile trucks but also increased the danger for destructive avalanches across the region. Snow started at all elevations Thursday evening with snow levels rapidly rising Friday morning.  Heavy, wet snow (Sierra Nevada Cement) fell across higher elevation areas as snow levels rose to 7000 feet over the region.  Heavy rainfall increased the possibility of flooding for lower elevation creeks, streams, along with urban drainage issues due to snow berms. Significant rises on mainstem rivers were monitored for flooding. Deep, cold snowpack absorbed most liquid precipitation during this second AR supported storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased southwest winds, especially Monday evening through Tuesday night which again increased the chance for possible travel disruptions, tree and fence damage as well as local power outages. Mountain snow showers increased intensity during the evening above 7000 feet dumping heavy wet snow over mountain communities which brought heightened snow load  concerns for those areas. With the attendant stronger AR bringing relatively higher snow levels and more heavy rain to an already primed heavy snowpack, flood concerns further increased for larger streams and creeks, as well as for mainstream rivers. Temperatures for the first March AR supported storm system remained mostly below seasonal averages, and contributed to limited flooding issues.  Temperatures for the subsequent two storm systems remained mostly near to slightly above average in response to the warmer tap of sub-tropical moisture. Therefore, flooding issues increased for these two follow up AR driven storm systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (NNSC-DRI) located 2.3 mi SSW of Sun Valley, NV, and mesonet station (DBYNV - I80 and Derby Dam) located 8.0 mi WSW of Wadsworth, NV, as well as Official NWS Observation  located 2.0 mi SW of Sun Valley, NV, reported peak wind gusts of 68 mph, 71 mph, and 70 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This AR fed winter system shaped up to be a major heavy snow event for Mono, Mineral, and Southern Lyon Counties,  that extended south into Inyo and Esmeralda Counties. A deformation axis wrapped into the region with moist subtropical air transported by a moderate to strong AR that was directed into central and southern California. A strong southeast to east-southeast upper flow also aided in setting up the convergence zone in addition to upslope flow into the eastern Sierra. Impacts were considerable for routes between western and southern NV, that included closures of US-395, US-95, and Hwy-6 due to heavy snow in addition to strong south-southeast winds creating blowing and drifting snow and periods as well as near zero visibility. Dangerous wind chills occurred across the High Sierra with additional impacts for more vulnerable populations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Diamond Peak Ski base and the Mount Rose Ski Base, as well as a trained spotter(WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village reported storm total snowfall of 11.0 inches, 10.0 inches,  and 6.8 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds 30-40 mph preceded the storm system and attendant cold front, bringing the increased potential for travel, aviation, and recreation impacts, especially across higher elevations of the eastern Sierra.  Snow began in the Sierra & northeast California Saturday morning with peak snowfall Saturday afternoon through evening, and again overnight into Sunday morning.  Sunday morning rain and snow began to spillover into western Nevada. Impacts included snow load on infrastructure, roofalanche potential,and travel /ski commute disruptions. Heavy, deep snow covering most road ways lead to extended road closures for many Mono county state highways and roads, especially along US 395 from Bridgeport to Mammoth Mountain valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, even damaging west-southwest winds increased travel hazards especially to high profile trucks but also increased the danger for destructive avalanches across the region. Snow started at all elevations Thursday evening with snow levels rapidly rising Friday morning.  Heavy, wet snow (Sierra Nevada Cement) fell across higher elevation areas as snow levels rose to 7000 feet over the region.  Heavy rainfall increased the possibility of flooding for lower elevation creeks, streams, along with urban drainage issues due to snow berms. Significant rises on mainstem rivers were monitored for flooding. Deep, cold snowpack absorbed most liquid precipitation during this second AR supported storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS, NV-CC-20 Carson City, located 3.6 mi NNW of Carson City, NV, reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 1.62 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle accident took place in the morning hours of the 10th along US-93 (MM45). All lanes of traffic were subsequently closed for 5 hours. No injuries or deaths were reported. US-93 was closed from the city of Wells, NV (MM73) south to Lages Junction (MM0), an approximate 80 mile stretch or road. Weather conditions were gusty with SE winds 20-30 mph with gusts up to 40 mph. The direction of the wind was blowing perpendicular to the roadway leading to possible blowing snow and reduced visibility. Decision support was given to DOT with a live weather briefing the day prior as well as partner emails in the days leading up to the event. A Wind Advisory was also in effect for the area. NDOT had extra snow removal equipment in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another AR enhanced winter storm brought travel impacts to the region later Monday night through Wednesday, with strong winds, heavy Sierra snowfall, and periods of moderate valley rain or snow. A boom-bust scenario was projected to express the uncertainty of the track of a surface low track along the California coast. If the low tracked more south and offshore, this would limit the amount of moisture and mesoscale lift necessary for heavier snowfall across the region and lead to the higher chance bust scenario.  The lower probability boom scenario included if the low tracked closer to the Sierra Nevada and crossed into western Nevada. This projected track was expected to lead to stronger frontogenesis and moisture flux that would generate more persistent bands of heavier snowfall. With the high spread of potential scenarios, the official forecast split the difference.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 8.0 mi SW of Lockwood, NV, 6.0 mi N of Washoe City, NV, and 4.0 mi SSW of Reno, NV reported a 24-hour snowfall total of 8.5, 8.0, and 6.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter AR driven storm brought stronger winds especially for mountain tops, heavy Sierra snowfall, and valley rain or snow showers through the forecast period. Travel impacts were also expected for regional roadways which included Sierra mountain passes to include the I-80 corridor and along US-50 east of US-95 as rain changed to snow after sunset. Strong southwesterly winds were ongoing across the region with gusts exceeding 100 mph along Sierra ridgelines and widespread gusts to 50 mph in lower western Nevada valleys. Mono County was expected to see the snow last up to 15 hours with the heaviest snow area generally directed along the Sierra for about 9-12 hours, resulting in a solid swath of 1-2 feet along the crest, with some higher Mono County peaks projected to receive 30-36 inches of new snow. Through this challenging forecast period, brief whiteout conditions along with blowing/drifting snow were expected across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public (social media) located at Dagget Pass, NV, reported 24-hour snowfall totaling 9.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper low shifting eastward out of region leaving a moist and unstable airmass. The anomalous higher PWATS (90-95th percentile) and CAPE favored scattered thunderstorms across the region with heavy rainfall cores capable of flash flooding, gusty and erratic outflow winds, small hail, and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MJBN2 - Majuba) located 21.0 WNW of Imlay, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought strong gusty winds and a modest cooldown through this period. These strong winds also brought periods of choppy-rough lake waters, bumpy turbulent air to aviation concerns, and strong cross winds to high-profiled vehicles traveling on area roadways. Unseasonable freezing morning temperatures returned to Sierra valleys the first half of the upcoming week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (Slide-DRI) located 1.0 mi E of Mount Rose Summit, CA, reported maximum wind gusts of 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that settled over southern NV/UT encouraged the return of greater thunderstorm coverage during this period  with near severe storms bringing heavy rain and flash flood concerns to the region. These strong storm cells also brought along gusty, erratic winds, small hail, and plenty of lightning. Slightly below normal temperatures continued before returning to near normal the middle of upcoming week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (FISN2-Fish Springs) located 5.0 mi ESE of Gardnerville, NV, reported a total rainfall amount of 0.96 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As plentiful moisture and jet energy streamed into the Pacific Northwest, unsettled weather continued across the Sierra and western Nevada. Periods of light showers and stronger westerly winds aloft allowed ridgeline winds to gust to 70 mph through this two day period.  Well not necessarily impactful, travel over the Sierra was difficult at times due to snow levels dropping with a slushy inch or two falling on regional mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 93 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern settled into the region through this period as the first of two cold fronts sweeps across the region bringing strong gusty west-southwest winds, as well as periods of heavy snowfall across the Sierra snow, and valley rain. A second weaker forced low pressure system swept through the region and brought another round of gusty winds and moderate snowfall to the Sierra above 5000���. The combined effects from both storms brought daytime and evening travel and recreation impacts to the region through this period.

Mesonet station (NV021-SW Gardnerville), and mesonet station (NV061-Wild Oat Mountain) located 1.0 mi SSE of Holbrook Junction, NV, both reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (GPEN2-Washoe City) located 3.0 mi NE of Mount Rose Ski Base, NV, reported a rainfall storm total of 0.55 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather warm low pressure system supported by a moderate AR flow (IVT: 400-600 kgm-1s-1) anchored in the eastern Pacific offshore the western US, and initiated a stormy pattern over the region. This system swung multiple short wave troughs into California and Nevada that produced widespread  valley rain, higher mountain snow, some periods of freezing rain across northeast California to include lower valley areas of the Basin and Range, and breezy south-southwest winds. As snow levels dropped over a 48 hour period, moderate to heavy higher elevation snowfall increased travel difficulties over higher Sierra passes from northeast California to the Lake Tahoe Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations (MRSN2) located at the Mount Rose Summit (SNOTEL), mesonet station (MRNL2) located 4.4 mi N of Spooner Summit, NV (SNOTEL), and mesonet station (SHEN2) located 5.0 mi NW of Catnip Mountain, NV (SNOTEL) reported 24hr rainfall storm totals of 1.2 inches, 1.0 inches, and 0.6 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base, NV, reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 6-10 inches at 8260 ft. The storm total snowfall amount was 8.0-11.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that settled over southern NV/UT encouraged the return of greater thunderstorm coverage during this period  with near severe storms bringing heavy rain and flash flood concerns to the region. These strong storm cells also brought along gusty, erratic winds, small hail, and plenty of lightning. Slightly below normal temperatures continued before returning to near normal the middle of upcoming week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNE of Hawthorne, NV, and an AWOS station (KHTH-Hawthorne Industrial Airport) located 1.0 mi NNW of Hawthorne, NV, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.96 inches and 0.74 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm shaped out to be the strongest of our recent series of late February winter storms. A low positioned off-shore the Pacific Northwest set a favorable pattern for the transport of colder northern Pacific moisture that wrapped into an attendant cold front. This storm possessed a combination of a cold air mass, sufficient moisture, strong winds, instability, and dynamic forcing to produce heavy Sierra snowfall with periods of blizzard conditions Monday through early Wednesday. With snow rations approaching 15-17:1, the light fluffy snowfall coupled with wind gusts exceeding 50 mph brought near zero visibility at times to Sierra communities Monday afternoon through Tuesday afternoon. Sierra ridge tops saw wind gusts over 100 mph through this period. This storm system also contributed to prolonged winter travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations located 2.0 mi NNE of Washoe City, NV, 2.0 mi SSE of Verdi, NV, and 1.0 mi SSW of Stead, NV, reported peak wind gusts of 59 mph, 60 mph, and 61 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An AR event brought high winds and snow transitioning to rain, resulting in some snowmelt and extensive flooding in the area of Eureka, NV. An estimated $7 Million in damages occurred with the flooding. Damage coasts have been estimated, and split between the 7 entries from Eureka County. The Yomba Tribe, near the town of Austin, also experienced flooding estimated to be around $5.5 Million in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yomba Tribe experienced flooding from warm temperatures causing rapid snowmelt. The tribe experienced sitting water around a lot of homes, as well as a few homes with flooded crawlspaces. Local dirt roads were also washed out cutting off access in and out of the area. Some fiber optic lines were also exposed due to erosion. The flooding occurred near the Reese River and Stewart Creek area. SR 21 had plenty of washouts and 4-5 feet of water in 1 particular area. SR 844 to the west also had plenty of washouts. The Reese River itself has only ran seasonally there in the last 10 years but suddenly rose to approximately 10 feet, nearly hitting the undertone of their bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused gusty winds, hail, and even a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado started around 1:40 PM PDT, south of Halleck, NV. At 2:00 PM PDT the first discernible damage path was observed at a nearby ranch southeast of Halleck. This tornado continued north and east, crossing SR-229 before dissipating around 2:10 PM PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low that settled into the west coast the first few days of May brought showers and an increased chance (20-30%) of thunderstorms that also lowered rain-snow lines to 5500-7000 feet by early morning 3 May. Stronger showers and storms produced a mix of rain, snow, and ice pellets which lead to runoff issues in the lower elevations. High end chances (10-20%) of rainfall totals were expected to reach 0.50-1.00 inch. Although snow was projected to fall in the Sierra, the combination of warm road temperatures and the early May sun angle limited accumulations on paved surfaces. But bursts of convective heavy snow showers and/or snow occurring during the overnight hours were expected to bring overnight travel impacts to mountain passes. Tightening thermal and pressure gradients with the incoming low produced gusty south-southeast winds and travel impacts for portions of Churchill, Mineral, Lyon, and Mono Counties along US-395 and US-95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS station (CA-NV-79 Reno), located 9.0 NNE of Cold Springs, NV, and the public located at Dagget Pass, NV, reported 24 hour snowfall totals of 12.0 inches and 3.0 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE)  located 0.9 mi ESE of the Mount Rose Summit (DRI), mesonet station (SRSNV) located 1.3 mi NW of Lakeview, NV, and mesonet station (GLCNV) located 2.3 mi NE of Washoe City, NV, reported maximum wind gusts of 121 mph, 73 mph and 64 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Uncertainty with the storm track of this particular winter storm system contributed to a variance of projected precipitation scenarios as this system dropped south near to  the California coast.  Yet enough moisture remained to produce some light snow in the Sierra and western NV.  Locally higher storm totals reached  to ????  inches.  Some travel impacts were incurred for area mountain passes and lower valley roadways overnight Friday into Saturday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COCORAHS  Station (NV-WH-79) located 9.0 mi NNE of Cold Springs Valley, COCORAHS  Station (NV-WH-166) located 3.2 mi NE of Sparks, NV,  and NWS Employee located 2.0 mi ESE of Spanish Springs, NV reported 24 hour snowfall totals of 5.7, 4.5, and 3.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with tight thermal and pressure gradients drove into the western US February 21 and brought mostly wind impacts to the region, as well as some accumulated snow for Sierra passes and western Nevada roadways overnight through the morning February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer located  at Virginia City, NV, reported a 24 hour snowfall total of 4.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder and wetter system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow and breezy conditions to the Sierra, northeast CA, and western NV.  Storm total snow across much of the Sierra and the Lake Tahoe basin saw a foot or more of accumulated snow. Accumulating snow fell along western Nevada foothills above 5000��� with a rain/snow mix for most  valleys.   Snow levels fell to valley floors after sunrise on Sunday, with scattered snow showers producing  quick bursts of snow during the remainder of the day region-wide.  Strong winds blasting along higher ridgetops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations (SLIDE), Mount Rose summit (DRI), located 1.0 mi ESE of the Mount Rose Summit area, and mesonet station (XWSH-Washoe Lake), located 2.0 mi SSW of New Washoe City, NV, reported maximum wind gusts of 114 mph and 62 mph respectively.

The Yomba Tribe, near the town of Austin, also experienced flooding estimated to be around $5.5 Million in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave swung south to north across the region that brought moisture/instability northward across much of  the Sierra and western NV.  This fostered increased chances (30-45%) for afternoon and evening showers/thunderstorms from I-80 southward. These storms brought lowered CIGS/VIS with terrain obscuration during heavier rainfall, gusty and erratic outflow winds with periods of blowing dust, frequent lightning and small hail. A handful of storms were expected to be on the drier side with lightning strikes outside of the wet cores on the northern edge of the moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm outflows caused a dust storm 9 mi SSW of Lovelock, NV, that reduced visibility to 1/4 mile at Derby Field with southeast winds gusting 30-40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low spinning offshore the California coast rotated moisture and instability northward along the Sierra to the Lake Tahoe Basin. This brought a 30-60% chance though the period for afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms that extended along the eastern Sierra-Mono County through the  Lake Tahoe Basin, then northward into northwest California. This also increased chances (15-30%) for storms across the Basin and Range as well. Stronger storms produced gusty and erratic outflow winds up to 40 kts with periods of blowing dust ablated off desert sinks and playas across the Basin and Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located 4.5 mi E Sutcliffe, NV, reported a maximum wind gusts of 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-376 was washed out at mile marker 62 north of the city of Cavers, NV. Anywhere from 1-4 inches of rain from Tropical Storm Hilary fell across Nye County. Quima Peak Raws, located just 35 miles south of the washout, measured 3.96 inches of rain from the event. SR-376 from Cavers to US-50 has been closed as a result and remains closed indefinitely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that stalled offshore the California coast injected inland as an open short wave trough and brought an increased chance for showers to the Sierra and Western Nevada. A subsequently stronger, more dynamic, upper shortwave and attendant cold front dropped south and southwest into the region and brought enhanced, more organized bands of shower activity, lower snow-levels, and breezy south-southwest winds. This resulted in some  travel impacts to higher Sierra passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base, NV, reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 6-10 inches at 8260 ft. The storm total snowfall amount was 8.0-11.0 inches.

An unsettled pattern established by a broad area of troughing over the western states that continued through the end of May. Upper level shortwaves provided sufficient forcing for generation on a near-daily basis of afternoon convection in the prevailing moist and unstable air-mass. A nearly zonal steering flow aloft led to faster moving and stronger storm cells that held the potential for outflow gusts to 50 mph, brief heavy rainfall, and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (MJBN2-MAJUBA) located 21.0 mi WNW of Imlay, NV, reported a thunderstorm peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over northern California yielded a cooler,  wetter, and breezy pattern across the region through Labor Day weekend. Periods of light to moderate precipitation intermingled with isolated thunderstorms produced brief periods of heavy rainfall across the Sierra into northwest Nevada that caused some flash flooding and flooding concerns through the Labor weekend. Of particular concern was flooding in the playa area of the Black Rock Desert during the Burning Man festival. A few isolated severe thunderstorms occurred across parts of the Basin and Range east of US-95 in Pershing, Churchill, and Mineral counties as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit reported a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed each afternoon during the weekend of 9-10 September, mainly over areas south of US-50. Subtropical moisture steadily flowing north along the western periphery of a longwave ridge centered over the US/Mexico border moistened mid-levels across southern portions of our CWA increased storm chances to 20-30%. Hazards included stronger outflow winds, frequent lightning, localized areas of heavy rainfall, and the increased chance for small hail in stronger storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relayed flash flood report from Mineral County Search and Rescue. A mixture of rocks, water, mud and debris of unknown depth closed NV-359 at milepost 9.  Event time based on a separate NDOT report of water on the road near the same location, or 22.0 mi S of Hawthorne, NV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought active weather into parts of northern California and western Nevada mostly north of US-50 during this period. A 15-20% chance for isolated thunderstorms A narrow band of showers along a cold front passage overnight Friday into Saturday morning was followed by a 15-20% chance for isolated thunderstorms Saturday afternoon. Then intermittent bands of valley rain/mountain snow showers followed later Saturday night through early Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (LVYN2-Little Valley) located 2.0 mi ESE of Diamond Peak Ski resort reported a storm total rainfall amount of 0.55 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of afternoon instability as well as increasing mid-level moisture pushing northward on the eastern flank of an upper level low that moved over SoCal and stalled over central-southern California. This upper feature introduced a daily chance of showers and thunderstorms during the first week of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (TORNV) located 9.8 NE of Trinity Junction (NV DOT) reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of cold fronts brought strong westerly winds on February 13 with winds turning more northerly Saturday. This double shot of winter weather impacted larger area lakes with rough, choppy conditions as well as very  turbulent conditions for aviation interests, and strong crosswinds for high profile vehicles traveling along area roadways.  Some snow showers developed across the area east of the eastern Sierra into western Nevada south of Highway 50. But snow accumulations were generally spotty, as well as  light to moderate, with limited travel impacts except for brief reductions in visibility in heavier showers. Area temperatures plunged behind the quickly moving cold fronts with daytime highs for warmer lower valley locations struggling to rise above freezing.  The colder, brisk northerly flow brought frigid wind chill temperatures overnight February 14 that dipped into the single digits for most valley locations. Higher Sierra elevations saw dangerous wind chills plunging into the  -20 to -30 degree through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI),  located 1.0 mi ESE of the Mount Rose Summit area, and mesonet station (WASNV-NVDOT), located 3.2 mi S of New Washoe City, NV, reported maximum wind gusts of 94 mph and 63 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample mid and upper level moisture that streamed across western Nevada, coupled with summer daytime heating, increased instability across the region that allowed for increased shower and thunderstorm chances (15-30%) that extended mostly south of US-50 from southern Mono County through Mineral counties. These storms were forecast to be a hybrid variety capable of gusty outflow winds up to 50 mph, heavy rainfall, and isolated dry strikes outside of storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along NV 338, which closed the highway from Wellington to the CA/NV border. Location is approximate based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample mid and upper level moisture that streamed across western Nevada, coupled with summer daytime heating, increased instability across the region that allowed for increased shower and thunderstorm chances (15-30%) that extended mostly south of US-50 from southern Mono County through Mineral counties. These storms were forecast to be a hybrid variety capable of gusty outflow winds up to 50 mph, heavy rainfall, and isolated dry strikes outside of storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NV-359 closed in Mineral County near mile post 25 due to flooding. Reported by NDOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate to strong southwest flow aloft and daytime heating enhanced afternoon and evening breezes with gusts in the 20-30 mph range with higher gusts in wind prone areas. These breezy conditions brought periods of isolated critical fire weather conditions, and choppy conditions across area lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE)  located 0.9 mi ESE of the Mount Rose Summit (DRI) reported a maximum wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low that settled over southern NV/UT encouraged the return of greater thunderstorm coverage during this period  with near severe storms bringing heavy rain and flash flood concerns to the region. These strong storm cells also brought along gusty, erratic winds, small hail, and plenty of lightning. Slightly below normal temperatures continued before returning to near normal the middle of upcoming week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 5.0 mi NNW of Spanish Springs, NV, a mesonet station (WRUN) located 6.7 mi N of Washoe City, NV,  a mesonet station (CW1177-Reno) located 3.0 mi ESE of Stead, NV, and a mesonet station (DESN2-Desert Springs) located 4.0 mi NW of Spanish Springs, NV, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.7 inches, 0.79 inches, 0.92 inches, and 1.02 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft between low pressure trough over the northeast Pacific and an upper ridge anchored over the Desert Southwest allowed mid-level moisture to stream north-northeast  over western Nevada. The increased precipitable water, coupled with daytime heating and steepened lapse-rates favored the chances (10-20%) for convective initiation with isolated thunderstorms forecasted east of US-395 into the Basin and Range, as well as, north of the Sweetwater Mountains to the Oregon border. Stronger outflow winds in the late afternoon through early evening produced periods of reduced visibility in blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MJBN2-Majuba) located 21.0 mi WNW of Imlay, NV, reported a maximum wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough passed through Nevada producing not only valley rain and mountain snow showers but also gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (ALLN2) Alligator Ridge Raws, Elevation 6675 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern settled into the region through this period as the first of two cold fronts sweeps across the region bringing strong gusty west-southwest winds, as well as periods of heavy snowfall across the Sierra snow, and valley rain. A second weaker forced low pressure system swept through the region and brought another round of gusty winds and moderate snowfall to the Sierra above 5000���. The combined effects from both storms brought daytime and evening travel and recreation impacts to the region through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported 24 hour storm snowfall totals of 13.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that was moving slowly east across northern California into the Great Basin late September into the early October brought increased shower and thunderstorm chances to northeast California, the Sierra Front and eastern Sierra to include parts of western Nevada. This included high elevation snow showers over parts of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (CW2857-Fernley) located 2.0 mi WSW of Fernley, NV reported a total rainfall amount of 0.50 inches.
